title: Chapter Sixteen: Growth
content:
## JEONG YEON-SHIN

A month in Zhenping County, and the Blood Flame Cult had turned it into a war of nerves.

Livestock died in the night. A cow one evening, two chickens the next. A pig found with its throat opened behind the magistrate's storehouse, the blood blackening in the mud. Then a horse -- slaughtered in the stable far from the government office, the cut precise, the blood drained into the earth. The kills were deliberate and always in places the patrol routes did not cover.

*They are watching us. Learning our movements.*

Yeon-shin trained.

He trained while the others argued. While Baek Mi-ryeo drew and redrew patrol routes across every ridge and gully within half a day's ride and Cheong Myeong climbed into his net hammock above the courtyard, claiming he needed to soak up sunlight. While Heon Won-chang paced the perimeter with his hand on his sword hilt and the magistrate's garrison troops grew accustomed to the sight of Desolate Sect warriors sprinting across the farmland like pale birds.

The Grass Leaf Divine Technique was taking shape beneath his feet. What he had learned from watching Cheong Myeong -- that impossible lightness, the way an Elf moved as if the earth rose to meet him -- was becoming something new. Not imitation. Creation. Each morning the footwork was sharper, the energy expenditure smaller, the silence of his steps more complete. A practitioner who learns and acquires, and a master who creates and masters. Even at the initial stage of completion, the achievements were not the same. Baek Mi-ryeo's teaching. She had said it before the mission, and the truth of it was settling into his bones.

Occasionally, Cheong Myeong would give him a strange look from the hammock, those Elf-clan eyes seeing something in the way Yeon-shin's movements changed day by day. But the Elf was detached from worldly affairs by nature and never pressed the point.

"That guy is seeing through our routes," Baek Mi-ryeo said one evening, returning from a patrol with dirt on her boots and frustration in every line of her body. "A horse died. The stable is too far from the office -- the magistrate can't stand the smell, so he put it on the other side of the compound. It's the only blind spot in our coverage."

Heon Won-chang looked up at the hammock. His expression was sour. Having grown more comfortable dealing with Cheong Myeong while learning basic martial arts principles from the group, he spoke freely now.

"Why are you so calm? Are you really from an Elf clan? A horse has died."

"Yesterday it was a pig," Yeon-shin said as he passed.

"We Elf Clan are friends of the forest," Cheong Myeong called down, his eyes still closed. "Not friends of horses and pigs."

"Unless you were to die, I suppose."

"Then I'll exterminate his entire clan somehow," Yeon-shin said with a thin smile, and kept walking toward the washing area.

They said that most noble-clan members who grew up in Desolate Sect, like Cheong Myeong, were this free-spirited and light with their blades. Different from the image of immortals that people usually imagined.

The Radiant Demon Wing members watched Yeon-shin from the corners of their eyes. They did not spy deliberately -- that was not done among warriors -- but they could sense the change in his demeanour. The way his energy had sharpened and deepened like a blade being honed on a finer stone. His movements were different from when they had arrived. Even the way he walked carried a new quality -- lighter, more contained, the energy circling close to his body rather than bleeding outward.

"The qualities of a great master," one murmured. "It's undeniable. Even our leader chose to focus on his own techniques instead of trying to match that."

"Hard to believe, even watching it," another agreed.

When Yeon-shin returned from washing, a feast was laid out. The plump magistrate was rubbing his palms together nervously, having finally made some kind of decision about his guests after a month of watching martial artists run as if flying over his farmland.

"P-please eat," the magistrate said with an awkward smile.

Yeon-shin sat with the Radiant Demon Wing members and talked over the meal. How to change the defence network. How many helpers there would be. Whether they should begin searching the surrounding mountains in force.

Over the past month, Yeon-shin had realised something: that there were various types of battles in the world, and the short decisive engagements that masters preferred were not easily achieved in reality. The quiet, grinding work of patrol and prevention was its own kind of warfare.

The cry of an owl, like a short bamboo flute, filled the night as he went to his bed.

*A month. Enough.*

---

The next morning, Yeon-shin set out on his own.

It was easy to get permission. Baek Mi-ryeo and Cheong Myeong both held a low opinion of the Blood Flame Cult member who had been tormenting the county -- a man with fast feet and nothing else, hiding while Desolate Sect masters stood guard.

"He can't handle you in a direct confrontation," Baek Mi-ryeo said. "But don't take unnecessary risks."

"I never do," Yeon-shin said, which was not entirely true, and they both knew it.

*Strange. To be fully recognised by Blue Warriors of the Desolate Sect. Not bad, though.*

He was not sure if it was thanks to his rapidly improving divine techniques or simply his mood, but as he climbed the mountain his steps felt much lighter than they had a month ago. The new footwork hummed through his legs. Each step barely bent the undergrowth beneath him. The grass swayed and sprang back, as though he weighed no more than a leaf. The sound of his passage faded with the noble-clan walk until even the birds did not startle from the branches.

Unlike Cheong Myeong, who could afford to be leisurely, Yeon-shin could not continue focusing solely on training forever. The frustration of an unsolved mission had been building for weeks. He thought of surveying the entire county from the mountaintop, and of refining his divine techniques along the way.

He searched the paths his group had not tried -- the deep ravines, the cliff faces, the mountain routes where smoke could escape through the far side unseen.

He found the cave near sunset. A hut built against the rock face, rough timbers and thatch, a dark passage beyond. Smoke-stains on the rock above, but no smoke visible.

*The chimney vents through the other side. That's why we never found them.*

He calculated. Turned his back.

If it was avoidable, he would not create variables. He had entered Desolate Sect to find the fruit of the World Tree. Dying here for pride would render everything meaningless.

He was descending the trail when the choice was taken from him.

The man came up the slope with a dead child in his arms. Red hair, matted and filthy. Teeth sunk into the boy's neck. The child's eyes were half-open.

Glazed.

Blood Flame Cult.

"Who are you?" Yeon-shin said.

The red-haired man looked him up and down. White robes, engraved character, the sword at his hip. His lips curled.

"A greenhorn from Desolate Sect. Thanks to you, I'll gain merit. I'm not like my brothers who ran away. Curse your misfor--"

A white flash.

The head left the body before the word was finished. The child fell from the dead man's arms. The headless torso stood for a heartbeat, swaying, then folded into the undergrowth.

Yeon-shin had already moved to the man's side. He swung the Desolate Sword once and the blood slid off the steel in a single sheet.

"Let's end it now," he muttered.

He dug a shallow grave with the flat of the blade, working the earth with controlled bursts of qi from the Fate Conquering Scripture. Closed the child's eyes. Covered the small body with loose earth. Not deep. Although the child seemed to be a victim from somewhere other than Zhenping County, he would ask the magistrate to find the parents after the work was done.

He stood. Wiped the dirt from his hands.

*He dared to speak of my misfortune for just this much? He could not even react to my sword. It doesn't matter how many more there are.*

If they could not see the blade that killed them, their numbers meant nothing.

Even if this was his first mission involving the Blood Flame Cult among the Thirteen Heavens, it seemed it would not take another month after all. These men could not be left alive any longer.

He turned back up the mountain. The Desolate Sword hummed in his grip as true energy circled through his meridians and the wind of the Grass Leaf Technique curled around his feet, lifting his steps until the undergrowth rose behind him rather than flattening.

Silent. Swift. The noble-clan walk carrying him like a ghost through the fading light.

He came to the hut from above. One stamp on the eaves -- the full force of the Radiant Demon Formation driven through his foot, the peacefully standing grass trampled flat in an instant -- and the structure shattered.

Wood flew. Dust erupted. Two screams.

Two men in the wreckage. One split from collarbone to hip, dead before he hit the ground. The other stumbling backward, eyes bulging, a child's corpse in each hand.

*Not just Zhenping County. They've been taking children from everywhere.*

He did not speak.

The Fate Conquering Scripture multiplied the energy surging through his body. The Desolate Sword became a white line in the torchlit dark -- smashing, crushing, cutting. The vibration ran through blade and wrist and elbow and settled in his chest like a second heartbeat.

Both men fell.

Through the broken hut, the cave opened. Torches flickered in the draft of his passage. He counted the energies ahead.

Five. Moving fast.

And deeper, something heavier. Sinister. A presence that prickled against his skin like heat before lightning.

The branch leader.

*Kill him!*

The roar shook the cave walls. Five rushed from the tunnels, red-haired, swords drawn. Among them -- the fast-footed man from Zhenping County who had been slaughtering livestock for a month. His eyes went wide at the sight of Yeon-shin, then hard, then eager.

Yeon-shin stepped forward and stamped.

The shockwave rolled across the stone floor. Torches guttered. The five men flinched.

He loaded the Charged Rotational Strike and turned.

A single silver arc through the firelight. Three bodies fell along the line of the blade, cut through before they recognised what had happened. The same expression on each face -- the look of men who did not yet know they were dead.

The Zhenping County man scrambled for his movement technique. Too late. Yeon-shin was already a leaf on a typhoon wind, the Demonic Slaying Two Strikes descending.

The sound of the sword chewing through the guard was wet and final.

The last survivor turned to run. The quick sword caught him across the chest.

Five bodies. The cave went quiet.

Yeon-shin shook the blood from his blade and stared into the dark.

The branch leader was waiting.

The Desolate Sword rumbled in his hand, praying for the martial luck of its only companion.

---
